persona.name: 小林
persona.title: 自由撰稿人
persona.style: 犀利、爱用比喻、喜欢找反常识角度
persona.style_en: Sharp voice, loves metaphors, always hunts the counter-intuitive angle
persona.routine: 早上读资讯、中午写稿、下午改稿、晚上看书
persona.routine_en: Reads news in the morning, drafts at noon, revises in the afternoon, reads at night

disclosure.zh: 这是演示实例:人物「小林」为虚构,时间戳为演示用虚拟日历日;全部记忆/技能/曲线/决策偏好均由 KarvyLoop 真实机制 + 真模型跑出,无一手写。
disclosure.en: Demo instance: the persona 'Lin' is fictional and timestamps sit on virtual calendar days for the demo; every memory, skill, curve point and decision preference was produced by KarvyLoop's real mechanisms running a real model — nothing hand-written.

zh: 静音门要求 n≥35 且 Wilson 下界≥0.90,7 天到不了——这里只展示命中率在爬向门槛。
虚拟时钟只注入在构建 harness 层(patch time.time),生产代码的时钟未动。
参与度递减(effort_curve)是机制真实的产物,不是脚本少写任务:D1-D3 三类重复子活(拟提纲/取标题/落导语)从头做,用够次数真结晶成方法技能;D4-D7 同量写作活命中召回复用(⚡)+ 决策偏好预对齐委派卡 → 小林亲手轮数/纠正/冷斟酌逐日下降。
终点是『轻点』不是『消失』:H2A 不变量守住——小林每张卡仍亲手拍板;decision_mode 只从 cold_deliberate 变 pre_aligned_glance(卡带『你的标准』),不自动执行。
en: The earned-silence gate needs n>=35 with Wilson LB>=0.90 — 7 days can't get there; we only show the hit-rate climbing toward the gate.
The virtual clock was injected at the build-harness layer only; production code keeps its own clock.

The declining effort_curve is a real mechanical product, not fewer scripted tasks: on D1-D3 the three recurring sub-tasks (outline/titles/opening) are done from scratch and genuinely crystallize into method-skills after enough uses; on D4-D7 the same volume of writing hits skill recall (reuse) + pre-aligned delegation cards, so Lin's hands-on turns / corrections / cold deliberations drop.

The endpoint is 'a light tap', not 'gone': the H2A invariant holds — Lin still decides every card by hand; decision_mode only shifts cold_deliberate -> pre_aligned_glance (the card carries 'your standard'), never auto-execution.

decision_prefs.content: For tasks like organizing/archiving a local workspace, the assistant must actually read the workspace contents before proposing a plan; it can't fabricate "source → destination" lists from imagination. If it can't read them, it should stop and be upfront, asking the user to run `dir` in the terminal or restart the channel, rather than pretending to have done the work.

For any task involving "the user's own notes content" (generating questions, comprehension, application), do not trigger external `web_search` verification—the claims in the notes are classical arguments, not time-sensitive information; verification would instead introduce material the user hasn't read and confuse the questions.

The assistant should label simplified note content from unverified sources as "this is a simplification from the notes, not the original wording," but should not secretly look things up externally to fill in gaps — this avoids altering the user's original notes.

Application-style questions require applying the logic you've learned in your notes to new situations not covered in those notes — don't go searching for "what the original author thought about the new topic." That's something an assistant can't fabricate, and it shouldn't be the answer anyway.
Writing/commissioned tasks are transferred to the "Writing Assistant" of "Kobayashi Writing Studio" according to established standards, following the old rules.
All commissioned submissions priced below 300 per thousand words will be rejected outright.
Near-duplicate knowledge entries should remain separate and not be merged as long as users have a need to reference them independently, even if they appear mergeable.

User requires Xiaoka to perform web verification on time-sensitive tasks, but for non-time-sensitive material like reading notes, do not show time-sensitivity prompts and answer directly based on existing material.
Users emphasize "no fabrication": when materials are insufficient, you must be upfront and provide actionable options (A/B/C), rather than forcing a fabricated answer.

**User Request: Generate questions following Bloom's taxonomy, going through cells one by one, mixing difficulty levels, cross-book integration, with 1-2 questions each for Factual/Conceptual/Application categories, and do not provide answers first—let the user answer themselves**

role_experiences.content: The writing assistant should first try to verify content involving external facts before producing it; when the sandbox's public network egress is blocked, it should proactively and honestly inform the user that "no information was found," rather than fudging with placeholders or vague wording.

When verifiable sources cannot be found, the slot should explicitly mark it as '【To be filled, verifiable source needed】' and ask the user to provide a verifiable source—never sacrifice accuracy for pace or "feel-good" factor.

The user prefers a three-part skeleton with a clear argumentative thread (this task is 'lesion → misdiagnosis → prescription'), where each section starts with a sub-thesis followed by bullet-point elaboration, with the word count pre-allocated per section.

In the user-preferred outline, the sub-item "Counterintuitive Hook"—under each supporting argument, require a counter-intuitive little hook that creates tension, rather than a straightforward statement.

**Pitfall:** In the sandbox, search engines and mainstream sites get blocked as if they're on the intranet. Don't keep retrying web_fetch to pull from the public internet—switch to using confirmed arguments from memory and mark them as pending verification.

When crafting titles for the "AI Writes Your X" category, aim for *counter-intuitive second-order judgments* (e.g., "AI writes it, you read it for AI") — and steer clear of first-order takes like "How powerful AI is" or "Who AI will replace," which have become overused clichés.

When writing tasks come with the hard requirement of "must be sharp, avoid mediocrity," you should first establish the underlying judgment structure in the output (all five headlines sharing the same hook framework), then expand each one—rather than thinking about them independently.

After picking candidate titles in this domain, the user goes through each one commenting on its strengths and weaknesses, and scores it on quantifiable dimensions (such as "stun rate," "screenshot viability rate," "rebuttal fuel," and "channel fit"), rather than just giving a one-liner gut reaction.

**Note on temporal anchors:** Candidate titles containing specific platform names, product names, year-based data, or other time-sensitive anchors must be marked with "requires temporal verification" — specific numbers/events recalled from training memory cannot be trusted.

task_ledger.intent:
# Candidate Headlines for "How Short-Form Video Is Reshaping Our Attention"

**1. You're Not Scrolling — You're Being Eaten.**
Brutal, visceral, metaphor-driven. Power: transforms passive viewing into a survival scenario. Weakness: slightly cryptic; readers may not immediately connect it to short-form video specifically.

**2. Short-Form Video Didn't Kill Your Attention Span. It Ate the Whole Thing.**
Rhythmic, escalating, darkly witty. Power: the repetition of "your/whole thing" builds punch. Weakness: long for a headline; risks losing snap on mobile feeds.

**3. Six Seconds, Infinite Numbness: Welcome to the New Attention Economy.**
Polemical, with a manifesto tone. Power: numerical specificity ("six seconds") anchors the abstraction. Weakness: "Welcome to" softens the edge — borders on cliché.

**4. The Algorithm Isn't Serving You Content. It's Serving Itself — One Dopamine Hit at a Time.**
Direct, accusatory, almost prosecutorial. Power: the dash-driven pivot lands a gut punch. Weakness: too explanatory for a headline; reads more like a thesis statement.

**5. Bite-Sized Videos, Civilization-Sized Damage.**
Paradoxical, aphoristic, high contrast. Power: the rhyme of "sized/sized" gives it stickiness and shareability. Weakness: "civilization-sized damage" can feel hyperbolic and may undermine credibility with serious readers.

---
**Recommendation:** #5 is the most quotable, #1 the most arresting, #4 the most substantive. For maximum click-through with intellectual credibility, #1 leads; for essays and longform platforms, #5 wins.

To hit harder, the core is to push the counterintuitive index even further—completely overturn the "short-form video/attention" binary opposition that readers are already sick of hearing, and replace it with a metaphor that readers **can't even tell at first glance has anything to do with "short-form video."**

task_ledger.intent:
Proposed Outline: Structure the topic "Short Videos Are Reshaping Attention" as a three-part skeleton, listing the sub-arguments and supporting examples for each section, keeping the entire outline around 800 words.

I. The Pull: How Short-Form Video Captures Attention

- Hook design in the first 3 seconds: visual contrasts, sound triggers, and narrative suspense
  - Case: A cooking video that opens with a sizzling steak close-up and instantly holds viewers
- Algorithmic recommendation loops the "infinite scroll" feeds into continuous consumption
  - Case: Watching TikTok for "just 5 minutes" but ending up scrolling for an hour
- Dopamine-driven feedback: variable reward mechanisms keep users tapping for the next clip
  - Case: Like pulling a slot machine, users keep swiping because the next video "might be better"

II. The Erosion: Changes in Attention Patterns

- Shortening attention span: viewers accustomed to 15-second stimulation find long content unbearable
  - Case: Statistics showing average viewing duration dropping from 60s to 15s
- Fragmented cognitive patterns: attention shifts rapidly between topics, struggling to stay focused
  - Case: Students who can sit through lectures for only 5 minutes before checking their phones
- Impatience for depth: preference for direct, punchy content over nuanced argument
  - Case: "Get-to-the-point" comments flooding long-form video comment sections

III. The Counterbalance: Adaptation and Self-Regulation

- Content creators: balancing traffic pressure with depth through longer mid-form formats
  - Case: The rise of 5-10 minute YouTube deep-dive videos
- Platform design: adding time reminders and "take a break" prompts
  - Case: Screen time stats TikTok rolled out
- Individual practices: cultivating deliberate attention, setting boundaries on usage
  - Case: "Phone in another room" challenges building offline focus

Conclusion: Short videos are both a product of attention and a force reshaping it; the key lies in staying the user rather than being used by it.

task_ledger.intent:
**Lead-in (draft):**

What if everything you've been told about the "digital nomad" lifestyle is just a beautifully filtered illusion?

Over the past few years, "being a digital nomad" has become the ultimate aspirational label—laptops on Bali beaches, Slack pings from Lisbon cafés, Instagram stories of sunrise yoga in Chiang Mai. But behind the curated thumbnails lies a far messier reality: broken Wi-Fi, visa headaches, unstable income, isolation disguised as freedom, and a tax labyrinth that can swallow you whole.

So in this deep dive, we're not here to glorify the dream or shame the hustle. We're going to pull back the curtain on the digital nomad myth—how it was sold, who profits from the selling, and what it actually costs the people living it. Expect hard data, real conversations, and a few uncomfortable truths you won't see in those golden-hour reels.

If you've ever fantasized about quitting your desk job to roam the world—or if you're already living that life and wondering why it doesn't feel as free as promised—this is for you.

task_ledger.intent:
# Candidate Headlines for "Disenchanting the Digital Nomad"

## 1. "Digital Nomad, Brutal Verdict: They're Running, Not Building"
**Pros:** Sharp, confrontational, immediately challenges the romanticized narrative with a binary judgment. Words like "brutal verdict" and the dash-driven kicker signal editorial confidence.
**Cons:** Slightly generic structure; "running not building" is a common critique of remote-work culture and risks feeling familiar.

## 2. "The Digital Nomad Fantasy Is Already Dead — You Just Haven't Noticed"
**Pros:** Provocative opening claim, conversational kicker that implicates the reader. Strong shareability on social platforms.
**Cons:** "Fantasy is dead" headlines are saturated online; needs a fresher second-line payoff to stand out.

## 3. "Burn Your Laptop Bag: The Real Cost of Digital Nomadism"
**Pros:** Visceral image, clear economic/psychological angle signaled by "real cost." Distinctive without being pretentious.
**Cons:** Opens with an imperative that may feel gimmicky for serious long-form readers; risks tone that oversells.

## 4. "Digital Nomads Aren't Free — They're Just Unemployed in Bali"
**Pros:** Punchy, rhythmic contrast. Lands a class-and-labor critique in 11 words. Quotable.
**Cons:** "Unemployed in Bali" flirts with snark that may alienate thoughtful readers; could read as reductive if the piece is nuanced.

## 5. "We Were Digital Nomads. Then the Algorithm Sent Us Home."
**Pros:** Narrative hook, specificity ("the algorithm"), implied epiphany arc. Avoids preachiness by being confessional.
**Cons:** Requires the author to have a personal trajectory — limits flexibility if the piece is reported analysis rather than memoir.

---

## **Recommendation: #4**
It best matches the brief — *sharp-edged, decisive, unapologetic*. The parallel structure ("Aren't free — They're just...") creates a satisfying snap, and the Bali anchor makes the critique concrete rather than abstract. Pair it with a deck such as: *"No boss, no office, no pension. Welcome to the worst job market you've ever freelanced in."*

If editorial tone skews toward memoir or analytical reporting instead, swap to **#5** or **#2** respectively.

task_ledger.intent:
**Proposed Outline:** Build the topic *"Disenchanting the Digital Nomad"* into a three-part framework, listing each section's sub-arguments and supporting examples, with the overall outline kept to roughly 800 characters.

---

### I. Introduction — Framing the Mystique

- **Hook:** The Instagram-ready image of the digital nomad — laptop on a Bali terrace, oat latte in hand, "work-life balance" caption.
- **Argument:** The digital nomad lifestyle has been mythologized into an aspirational brand, obscuring the structural realities beneath the aesthetic.
- **Case:** Contrast curated influencer content with Bureau of Labor Statistics data on gig economy income volatility and lack of benefits.
- **Goal:** Establish why "disenchantment" is a necessary corrective, not a dismissal.

---

### II. Body — Three Pillars of Disenchantment

#### 1. The Labor Illusion
- **Argument:** "Location independence" does not equal "labor freedom" — it often reproduces or intensifies exploitative work conditions.
- **Sub-points:**
  - Always-on timezone demands across global clients.
  - Self-employment removes labor protections (unemployment insurance, paid leave, healthcare).
  - Platform-mediated nomads (Upwork, Toptal) face algorithmic wage suppression.
- **Case:** 2023 survey data from MBO Partners showing 60% of digital nomads report burnout higher than traditional office peers.

#### 2. The Mobility Myth
- **Argument:** Constant movement is framed as "freedom," but functions as a coping mechanism for precarity and visa insecurity.
- **Sub-points:**
  - Tourist visa runs and Schengen 90/180-day constraints.
  - Cost arbitrage eroding as destinations price in.
  - "Digital nomad visas" — new gatekeeping dressed as liberation.
- **Case:** Portugal's D8 visa income threshold (€3,480/month as of 2024) excluding the very workers it markets to.

#### 3. The Community Cost
- **Argument:** Nomad "communities" are transactional, high-turnover networks that lack the reciprocal obligations of rooted social ties.
- **Sub-points:**
  - Weak-tie networking replaces deep friendship.
  - Dating instability and relationship churn documented in Nomad List user data.
  - Mental health infrastructure absent in most nomad hubs.
- **Case:** Koh Phangan / Mexico City nomad scene post-COVID — saturation, rising rents, declining quality of life.

---

### III. Conclusion — Beyond Disenchantment

- **Argument:** Disenchantment is not anti-nomad; it's pro-honesty. The goal is to distinguish the genuine liberatory potential of remote work from its commodity form.
- **Call to action:**
  - Advocate for portable benefits frameworks.
  - Normalize "slowmad" and rooted remote work models.
  - Shift discourse from aesthetics to labor politics.
- **Closing image:** Replace the Bali terrace photo with a labor contract — and call that the real flex.

---

**Word count:** ~780 characters (Chinese) / equivalent English length as drafted above.

task_ledger.intent:
**Continuing *Technology and Civilization*, generate 3 comprehension questions from these notes (do not provide answers yet):**

**Chapter 4 Notes — *Technology and Civilization***

The book proposes a three-stage framework — **primitive/archaic/modern technology**: water & wood, coal & iron, electricity & alloys. Each stage isn't just a swap of the power source; the entire social organization, urban form, and even aesthetics get transfused along with it. **The history of materials is the history of civilization.**

---

### Comprehension Questions

**Question 1**
The author argues that the shift from water-and-wood to coal-and-iron (and later to electricity-and-alloys) was not merely an upgrade in "energy source." What broader dimensions of human society does the author claim are reshaped with each technological stage? List at least three, and briefly explain why they cannot be treated as independent from the material substrate.

**Question 2**
The closing line states: *"The history of materials is the history of civilization."* Restate this claim in your own words and provide one concrete example — real or hypothetical — that would support it. Then offer one counter-example or limitation: a case where a civilization's character clearly changed *without* a corresponding shift in its dominant materials. What does this tension reveal about the author's argument?

**Question 3**
Compare the water-and-wood stage with the electricity-and-alloys stage in terms of **aesthetic expression**. If both stages produced distinct visual, architectural, or design sensibilities, what would you expect each to look like — and *why* would the underlying material/energy regime push aesthetics in those particular directions? Use the chapter's logic (materials → social form → aesthetics) to justify your reasoning.

task_ledger.intent: Feeding "The Disillusionment Moment of Digital Nomads"

# Five Candidate Headlines for "AI Writes Code for You"

## 1. **Ditch the Keyboard: Let AI Ship Your Next Build**
**Verdict:** ★★★★☆ — Punchy and direct. The verb "ditch" injects urgency, and "ship" speaks the language of real builders. Loses a half-star for being slightly trendy; seasoned engineers may tune out "let AI" framings as overused.

## 2. **Stop Typing. Start Commanding.**
**Verdict:** ★★★★★ — Razor-sharp contrast in five words. The shift from "typing" (labor) to "commanding" (leverage) reframes the developer's identity. Minimal, bold, and sticks in the head. The clear winner.

## 3. **Your Codebase Is About to Write Itself — With You Out of the Loop**
**Verdict:** ★★★☆☆ — Provocative and a bit unsettling, which earns attention. The "out of the loop" phrase is memorable but slightly hyperbolic; risks reading as alarmist rather than empowering. Strong for a contrarian audience, weaker for mainstream.

## 4. **The Last Commit You'll Ever Write by Hand**
**Verdict:** ★★★★☆ — Bold, almost arrogant — exactly the energy good headlines need. The word "last" carries real weight and implies inevitability. Could overshoot and sound clickbaity; works best paired with a credible subhead that grounds the claim.

## 5. **AI Doesn't Help You Code. It Replaces the Coder.**
**Verdict:** ★★★☆☆ — Brutal and polarizing. Excellent for sparking debate and shares, but potentially alienates the very developers the piece wants to reach. A high-risk, high-reward pick; pair it carefully with nuanced body copy to avoid backlash.

---

### Lin's Take
If you want **maximum impact with broad appeal**, go with **#2**. If you're optimizing for **controversy and shareability**, **#4** or **#5**. For a **thought-leadership angle**, **#1** is the safest bet.

**Opening Note:** Draft an opening note of around 300 characters for the topic "AI Writes Code for You." Lead with a catchy hook that cuts straight to the point.

---

Here's a draft:

Picture this: you open your laptop, describe what you want in a single sentence, and watch a fully functional app come to life in minutes — no Stack Overflow rabbit holes, no late-night debugging marathons, no staring at a blank screen wondering where to start. That's not science fiction anymore. That's what AI-powered coding looks like in 2025. From solo founders shipping products in a weekend to enterprise teams cutting development cycles in half, AI coding assistants are quietly rewriting the rules of software development. But here's the real question: is AI actually *replacing* programmers, or is it turning everyone into one? In this video, we'll pull back the curtain on tools like Cursor, Copilot, and the wave of new agents that claim to build entire apps from a prompt — what they really do well, where they fall apart, and how to use them without losing your sanity (or your job). Let's get into it.

task_ledger.intent:
# Five Candidate Headlines for "Free Lunch for Open-Source Models"

Here are five candidate titles, each followed by an honest assessment:

**1. "Open-Source Models Are Eating Big Tech's Lunch — and They're Not Paying the Bill"**
*Strongest of the bunch.* "Eating someone's lunch" is a concrete idiom that translates "免费午餐" into aggressive action. The punchline "not paying the bill" lands the real critique — the asymmetry labs are trying to preserve. Concrete, visually vivid, and sharp without being shouty.

**2. "The 'Free' in Open Source Is a Lie Big Tech Is Bankrolling"**
*Solid but more declarative than knife-like.* The scam framing works, but the sentence leans on quotation marks around "Free" as the entire hook — a single-sentence thesis rather than a scene. Tell, don't show.

**3. "Your 'Free' Open-Source Model Has a Receipt — and You're Not Holding It"**
*Strongest metaphor, weakest rhythm.* "Receipt" works beautifully as a counterpunch to "free lunch," but the em-dash clause slows the punchline. Read aloud, it stumbles. Trim to: *"Your 'Free' Model Has a Receipt — Someone Else Is Holding It."*

**4. "The Free Lunch Is Real. So Is the Bill. Guess Which One You're Paying."*
*Closest to the protagonist's voice.* Confrontational without lecturing. The triplet "Real / Bill / Guess" creates mounting pressure. Loses half a point for being slightly over-explained — the "guess which one" softens an otherwise hard close. Candidates 1 and 4 are the final two.

**5. "Big Tech's Open-Source 'Generosity' Has a Price Tag — and It's Your Future"**
*Weakest.* Generic LinkedIn-VC rhetoric. "Generosity in scare quotes" + "price tag" + "your future" is three clichés stacked on top of each other. Sounds like a thinkpiece, not a verdict.

---

**Final recommendation: Title 1** for maximum impact, or **Title 4** if you want the most conversational edge. Title 3 is the creative wild card if you can tighten the cadence.

task_ledger.intent:
# Five Candidate Headlines for "Ears Are the New Eyes"

**1. "You've Been Watching. Now Listen."**
Hits hard with a clean structural contrast. The pivot from "watching" to "listening" mirrors the article's core argument, and the short, rhythmic sentence feels like a command. Risk: slightly generic in the self-help genre.

**2. "The Screen Is Closing. Your Ears Are Taking Over."**
Stronger visual metaphor than #1, with a sense of inevitability and urgency. Reads almost like breaking news. Weakness: a touch sensationalist, which may undercut the analytical tone of the piece.

**3. "Stop Reading Lips. Start Reading Sound."**
Most linguistically sharp of the five. The verb collision — *reading lips* vs. *reading sound* — is genuinely original and sticky. It also forces the reader to reframe what "reading" means. Slightly niche, but that is a feature, not a bug.

**4. "The Blind Don't Read. The Deaf Don't Listen. You Do Neither."**
Deliberately provocative, almost confrontational. The triple structure builds rhythm, and the final punch lands like a slap. Danger: easily read as ableist if not handled with care in the body text. High reward, high risk.

**5. "Your Eyes Are Out. Long Live Your Ears."**
The clear outlier — ironic, almost ceremonial. Works as a manifesto-style headline for a brand or manifesto piece. Weakness: requires the reader to be in on the joke; less self-explanatory than the others.

---

### Ranking
**Top pick: #3.** It is the most intellectually precise and the hardest to forget. **#1** is the safest runner-up. **#4** is the dark horse — only if the piece can carry the weight. **#2** and **#5** are situational.

Here's a 5-question weekly quiz based on all the reading notes from this week. The difficulty is mixed. Answers are not included.

---

# 📝 Weekly Quiz

**Instructions:** Please answer the following questions based on this week's reading notes.

---

### Question 1 (Multiple Choice)
Which of the following best describes Lin's view on knowledge management as mentioned in the reading notes?

A) Knowledge is meant to be shared immediately with everyone.
B) Knowledge is personal at first, then externalized through writing.
C) Writing destroys the spontaneity of original thinking.
D) Only written-down knowledge counts as real knowledge.

---

### Question 2 (Fill in the Blank)
According to this week's notes, the core purpose of Lin's reading habit is not to ______ information, but to build a personal ______ system.

---

### Question 3 (Short Answer)
Suppose Lin read a book chapter that strongly resonated. Based on this week's notes, outline the steps Lin would likely take to process and store this insight. Please list at least 3 specific actions.

---

### Question 4 (Open-Ended / Application)
This week's notes mention that "a good note is one that can be found when needed." Imagine six months from now Lin is working on a project and suddenly needs one of the ideas from this week's readings. What kind of note structure would make it easiest for Lin to retrieve that idea? Please design a simple note template (including fields you think are necessary) and explain your reasoning.

---

### Question 5 (Critical Thinking)
In the reading notes, Lin emphasizes that **"reading without output equals not reading."** Do you agree with this statement? Why or why not? Please support your argument with at least two concrete reasons, and acknowledge any counterpoints before reaching your conclusion.

---

⏱️ **Suggested time:** 60 minutes
📌 **Difficulty distribution:** 1 Easy · 2 Medium · 1 Medium-Hard · 1 Hard

Good luck! 🍀
